original_hypothesis.title: VoteWeighting changesSum Underflow DoS After Nominee Removal
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: After removeNominee zeroes a nominee's bias but does NOT clean up future scheduled slope changes in changesSum/changesWeight, the _getSum() internal function can underflow when processing those residual slope decrements. Since _getSum() is called by voteForNomineeWeights, checkpointNominee, and nomineeRelativeWeightWrite, the entire VoteWeighting system (and thus staking incentive distribution) can be permanently DoS'd.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: In removeNominee, pointsSum[nextTime].bias is set to newSum but pointsSum[nextTime].slope is NOT adjusted. Users who voted for the removed nominee have future changesSum entries (at their lock expiration). When _getSum() processes weeks where bias reaches 0 (resetting slope to 0 via the if/else guard), subsequent changesSum decrements try to subtract from zero slope, causing Solidity 0.8+ underflow revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getSum() performs pt.slope -= dSlope without underflow protection; removeNominee zeros bias but not slope, leaving residual changesSum entries from unrevoked votes

preconditions: A nominee with active votes is removed via removeNominee
Enough time passes for bias decay to zero slope via the if/else guard in _getSum
A changesSum entry from an unrevoked vote tries to subtract from now-zero slope

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _getSum: pt.slope -= dSlope where dSlope includes removed nominee voter slopes, but pt.slope was zeroed when bias hit 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner removes nominee (normal admin action); users naturally fail to revoke votes; system DoSes itself over time
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit - this is a protocol-breaking DoS that halts all staking incentive distribution and voting

economic_questions: How quickly does bias decay reach zero after nominee removal?
What percentage of users typically revoke votes from removed nominees?
Can the DoS be triggered intentionally by an attacker who votes then doesn't revoke?
validation_requirements: Simulate: add nominee, vote, remove nominee, advance time past lock expiration, call _getSum
Verify that pt.slope -= dSlope reverts when slope was zeroed by bias decay
Test if revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower by ALL voters prevents the issue

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal veOLAS to cast a vote (~100 OLAS locked)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for voting + waiting for admin to remove nominee
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; protocol-wide disruption of all staking incentive distribution

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After any nominee removal where voters don't revoke
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete halt of staking incentive claims across all chains

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All veOLAS voters and staking incentive claimants
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete inability to vote or claim staking incentives
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire staking incentive allocation per epoch

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace _getSum through weeks after nominee removal with unrevoked votes

validation_result.mechanism: changesSum residual slope underflow after nominee removal
validation_result.impact_summary: After removeNominee zeroes bias without adjusting slope or cleaning changesSum entries, _getSum() can permanently revert due to arithmetic underflow, halting all VoteWeighting operations and staking incentive distribution.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The removeNominee function subtracts the removed nominee's bias from pointsSum but critically does NOT subtract the removed nominee's slope from pointsSum[nextTime].slope, nor does it clean up changesSum entries at voters' lock expiration times. This creates a state inconsistency. The excess slope in pointsSum causes the bias to decay faster than it should, eventually hitting zero and triggering the if/else guard that zeros slope. If new votes are subsequently cast (adding fresh bias and slope), the system enters a state where pt.slope only reflects new voters' slopes, but old changesSum entries from unrevoked votes on the removed nominee still exist. When _getSum() processes a week containing such a residual changesSum entry, it attempts pt.slope -= dSlope where dSlope exceeds pt.slope, causing a Solidity 0.8+ checked arithmetic underflow revert.

The concrete attack path:
1. Nominee A has active votes from User X (lock end T_end)
2. Owner removes Nominee A → changesSum[T_end] still has User X's slope, pointsSum slope still includes it
3. Excess slope causes pointsSum bias to decay to 0 → slope zeroed by if/else guard
4. User Y votes for Nominee B → pointsSum bias = newBias, slope = slope_Y only
5. When _getSum() reaches T_end: tries pt.slope -= changesSum[T_end] where changesSum[T_end] includes User X's slope > pt.slope → REVERT

Since _getSum() is called by checkpoint(), checkpointNominee(), nomineeRelativeWeightWrite(), and voteForNomineeWeights(), this permanently DoSes the entire VoteWeighting contract and all downstream Dispenser staking incentive calculations.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in removeNominee which sets pointsSum[nextTime].bias = newSum but does NOT modify pointsSum[nextTime].slope or any changesSum entries. The _getSum() function uses unchecked subtraction (pt.slope -= dSlope) within the if branch (pt.bias > dBias), which reverts on underflow in Solidity 0.8+. The revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower function exists as a mitigation but requires ALL voters to call it, which is unrealistic.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When pt.slope has been zeroed by the if/else guard (bias hit 0 due to excess slope from removed nominee) and then restored with only new voters' slopes, residual changesSum entries from unrevoked votes on removed nominees cause pt.slope -= dSlope to underflow.

removeNominee sets pointsSum[nextTime].bias = newSum (correctly removing nominee's bias) but does NOT set pointsSum[nextTime].slope to remove the nominee's slope contribution, nor does it clean up any changesSum entries at voters' lock expiration times. This creates a slope/bias inconsistency.

calling_sequence: 1. Owner calls removeNominee(account, chainId) for nominee with active votes
2. _getWeight and _getSum checkpoint to current time, filling pointsSum[nextTime]
3. pointsSum[nextTime].bias = newSum (reduced), but .slope is NOT reduced
4. changesSum entries at voters' lock end times are NOT cleaned up
5. Time passes: excess slope causes pointsSum bias to decay to 0 in _getSum
6. if/else guard zeros pt.slope when bias <= dBias
7. New votes are cast via voteForNomineeWeights, adding fresh bias and slope to pointsSum
8. _getSum() is called (via any entry: checkpoint, checkpointNominee, voteForNomineeWeights, etc.)
9. Processing reaches week with residual changesSum entry from removed nominee's voter
10. pt.bias > dBias is TRUE (new votes keep bias positive), so code enters if-branch
11. pt.slope -= changesSum[T_end] where changesSum[T_end] > pt.slope → UNDERFLOW REVERT
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: removeNominee leaves pointsSum.slope intact and changesSum entries intact. When _getSum() processes weeks between the slope-zeroing event and new votes, the residual changesSum entries create an underflow condition. Specifically: pointsSum[nextTime].slope after new votes only includes new voters' slopes, but changesSum[old_voter_lock_end] still contains the old voter's slope from the removed nominee. The subtraction pt.slope -= dSlope at VoteWeighting.sol:~210 reverts.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is realistic and requires minimal capital. An attacker needs only enough veOLAS to cast a single vote for a nominee. After the admin removes that nominee (a normal governance action), the attacker simply does NOT call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower. Over time, as the pointsSum bias decays to zero (due to the excess slope), the slope is zeroed. Once any other user casts a vote (adding new bias/slope), the system is primed for the underflow. When _getSum() eventually processes the week containing the old changesSum entry, it reverts permanently. This can also happen organically without any malicious intent if voters simply forget or are unaware they need to revoke.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Once triggered, _getSum() permanently reverts, which blocks: (1) voteForNomineeWeights - no more voting, (2) checkpoint/checkpointNominee - no weight checkpointing, (3) nomineeRelativeWeightWrite - no weight calculations, (4) Dispenser.calculateStakingIncentives (calls checkpointNominee) - no staking incentive distribution. The entire protocol's staking incentive distribution is permanently halted until the VoteWeighting contract is replaced.
recommended_mitigations: In removeNominee, subtract the removed nominee's slope from pointsSum[nextTime].slope: pointsSum[nextTime].slope -= pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].slope (compute before zeroing nominee weight)
In removeNominee, iterate through all voters of the removed nominee and clean up their changesSum entries at lock end times
Alternatively, use unchecked arithmetic with a floor of 0 in _getSum: if (pt.slope >= dSlope) { pt.slope -= dSlope; } else { pt.slope = 0; }
Add an admin function to manually clean changesSum entries for emergency recovery
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VoteWeighting with mock veOLAS. 2. Add nominee A. 3. Lock tokens in veOLAS for User X with lock end in 10 weeks. 4. User X votes for nominee A with weight 10000. 5. Advance 1 week. 6. Owner calls removeNominee(A). 7. Advance time until pointsSum bias decays to 0 (excess slope causes fast decay). 8. User Y locks tokens and votes for a new nominee B. 9. Advance time to User X's lock end week. 10. Call any function that triggers _getSum() (e.g., checkpoint). 11. Observe revert due to underflow in pt.slope -= dSlope.
uncertainties: The exact timing of when bias decays to zero depends on the relative magnitudes of the removed nominee's weight vs. total weight, and the removed nominee's voters' slopes vs. total slopes
If ALL voters who voted for the removed nominee call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower before the underflow condition is reached, the issue is prevented - but this is unrealistic to expect
additional_testing_needed: Foundry/Hardhat test with concrete values to demonstrate the underflow revert
Test that revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower by all voters prevents the issue
Test with multiple removed nominees to verify compounding effects
Test timing sensitivity - how quickly after removal can the DoS be triggered depending on weight distribution
